title: UConn Advances To Third National Title Game In Four Years
summary: UConn defeated Illinois 71-62 in the Final Four on Saturday night to make their third national title game in four years. Tarris Reed Jr. lead the Huskies with 17 points. Braylon Mullins added 15 points, going 4-7 from three. UConn lead by as much as 14 points in the second half before Illinois cut the lead to four points with less than five minutes remaining. The Huskes took a ten point lead into halftime while also not committing a turnover in the first half. Illinois finished the game at 106 points per 100 possessions, almost 30 points below their season average. The last time a team won three national titles in four seasons was John Wooden's UCLA team in 1975.

title: Lauren Betts, UCLA Defense Beat Texas In Low-Scoring Final Four Semifinal
summary: UCLA ended Texas's tournament run Friday in a grinding 51-44 semifinal victory, securing the program's first-ever NCAA championship game berth. The Bruins will face South Carolina on Sunday. Lauren Betts delivered 16 points and 11 rebounds to pace UCLA. The win avenged the Bruins' only defeat of the season, a 76-65 loss to Texas on Nov. 26, capping a 30-game winning streak since that setback. "All you can ask for is an opportunity to play your best basketball for a national championship," UCLA coach Cori Close said. Texas star Madison Booker finished with a season-low six points on 3-of-23 shooting. Her 17 consecutive missed attempts set a Final Four record for the longest individual scoring drought. "We couldn't make a shot tonight, and that's been my fear the last three days," Texas coach Vic Schaefer said. "I think we feel like in our locker room, we let one get away. I think this one will haunt me as the coach for probably 'til the day I die." The game ranked among the lowest-scoring in Final Four history, with both teams combining for fewer than 100 points, only the third time that has occurred. UCLA committed 23 turnovers, the most in a Final Four since 2008. Texas shot 30.8% from the field. A 7-0 fourth-quarter surge, sparked by a Kiki Rice three-pointer and a Gabriela Jaquez layup, pushed UCLA's lead to double digits. Texas trimmed the gap to 47-44 with 55 seconds left before Betts rejected a Booker layup attempt with 20 seconds remaining, effectively closing the door. "Last year we took that loss really hard," Betts said. "I'm just really proud of the growth and the way that we've held such a high standard consistently this year."

title: Steph Curry Says Managing Knee Injury Is 'New Normal'
summary: Golden State Warriors guard Steph Curry discussed his frustration in dealing with an "unpredictable" knee injury ahead of his expected return on Sunday against the Houston Rockets. Curry has missed 27 games with "runner's knee." "I thought I was going to be out a week," Curry said. "Ten days max. [But] every time I got on the court or tried to push it in that first month, there was always a reaction. You just knew it wasn't healing as fast as you thought." Curry, who completed a 5-on-5 scrimmage, was cleared by the medical staff to play on Sunday. The 38-year-old stated that managing the knee would be a "new normal" as he continues his career. "Yes and no," Curry said. "There's nothing structurally wrong with my knee. So it's not like I'm compromised out there. It is a new normal, though, if that makes sense." Curry added that dealing with the knee was different from previous injuries because there was no set timetable. "Hopefully win two play-in games," Curry said about his expectations for the rest of the season. "Then we can have another conversation."

title: Candace Parker, Amar'e Stoudemire, Doc Rivers Headline 2026 Hall Of Fame Class
summary: Candace Parker, Amar'e Stoudemire, and Doc Rivers were announced as part of a nine-member class set to be inducted into the Basketball Hall of Fame this upcoming August. They will be joined by Elena Delle Donne, Mark Few, Joey Crawford, Chamique Holdsclaw, Mike D'Antoni and the 1996 United States Women's National Team. "It means so much, just to be in a club of people that I've always admired and looked up to and have paved the way. I think it's super special," Parker said. Stoudemire, who was the only NBA player inducted in the class, also expressed his appreciation at the honor. "It means everything from a basketball point of view," Stoudemire said. "When you look back at my career not only as a pro, but even high school and, and nationally, and since I was a kid playing the game of basketball. I love the game. You play the game because you love it, and then you work to become the best you can possibly be, despite circumstances, and to get into the Hall of Fame, it shows that the voters appreciate it. And now we're enshrined forever." Parker is a three-time WNBA champion and two-time league MVP. Stoudemire was a six-time All-Star, five-time All-NBA selection, and the Rookie of the Year in 2002-03. Few has been the head coach at Gonzaga since 1999. He has 773 wins and two national title game appearances. Rivers won a NBA title in 2008 and ranks sixth in career wins for head coaches. Delle Donne is a two-time WNBA MVP and won a title in 2019. Crawford, who refereed 2,561 regular season games and 374 playoff games, is the 19th ref to be inducted into the Hall of Fame. The 1996 U.S. Women's National Team won the Olympic gold medal and featured ten future Hall of Famers. Holdsclaw was a six-time WNBA All-Star and three-time NCAA champion. D'Antoni was a two-time NBA Coach of the Year.

title: Joel Embiid Expresses Frustration Missing Wednesday Game: 'I Wasn't Allowed To Play Basketball'
summary: Joel Embiid voiced his frustration over missing the Philadelphia 76ers' 153-131 win over the Washington Wizards on Wednesday. Embiid missed the game due to a reported illness. "I was pissed off," Embiid said Friday. "I wanted to play basketball. I wasn't allowed to play basketball. So, this is more of a question for Daryl Morey." Embiid, who admitted that he was sick leading up to the game, said he was caught "off guard" by being held out. "I felt better going into Washington," said Embiid. "I didn't make it to shootaround. But when I found out after that I wasn't playing, it caught me off guard. It pissed me off. But it doesn't matter what I think. I have to follow directions." Team officials made the decision to hold Embiid out of the game as he had been sick for three days and missed the shootaround. Embiid returned to the Sixers' lineup on Friday night in a 115-103 win over the Timberwolves. Embiid finished with 19 points, 11 rebounds, and seven assists.

title: Cooper Flagg On Scoring Career-High 51 Points: 'The Basket Feels Big'
summary: Cooper Flagg scored a career-high 51 points in the Dallas Mavericks' 138-127 loss to the Orlando Magic on Friday night. Flagg, who shot 19-of-30 from the field, also became the youngest player in league history to score 50 points. "It's always fun getting into that type of mode," said Flagg after the game. "The basket feels big. Your teammates are looking out for you, helping you out. But I love to win, so that was my main focus. It's hard for me to fully enjoy myself out there when we're down 20, down 10, down 15 for the majority of the game." Assistant coach Frank Vogel, who took over after Jason Kidd was ejected arguing a no-call on Flagg earlier in the game, initially took Flagg out when he had 45 points with just under four minutes left. Flagg then returned to the game, topping his previous career-high of 49 points. Flagg also scored 24 points in the fourth quarter. Kidd threw in his support for Flagg being named Rookie of the Year after the game. "He should be Rookie of the Year. It's unbelievable," Kidd said. "The country is not watching the same thing that we get to watch on a daily basis. The things that he's done, he's in rare air. He's with the GOAT when you talk about MJ and what he did in his rookie year -- and as a teenager." Flagg is averaging 20.8 points, 6.6 rebounds and 4.5 assists on the season.

title: NBA Investigating Bucks Over Giannis Antetokounmpo Availability Dispute
summary: The NBA is investigating the Milwaukee Bucks over their handling of the player participation policy and potentially inconsistent statements regarding Giannis Antetokounmpo's health, sources told ESPN's Shams Charania on Friday. The league has already interviewed Antetokounmpo, the Bucks organization and team doctors. At the center of the dispute is a direct contradiction as Antetokounmpo has told both the Bucks and the NBA he wants to play, but the team will not medically clear him. Milwaukee, meanwhile, has informed the league it does not believe Antetokounmpo is genuinely ready or willing to return, sources told Charania. Antetokounmpo spoke directly to the conflict before Friday's game in Milwaukee. "I'm available to play, but I'm not in the game," he told reporters. "I'm available to play today. Right now. I'm available." He went further when addressing the organization's position. "For somebody to come and tell me to not play or not to compete, it's like a slap in my face," Antetokounmpo said. "So, I don't know where the relationship goes from there." Antetokounmpo has missed 10 consecutive games with a hyperextended left knee and bone bruise suffered March 15 against Indiana. Sources told Charania shortly after the injury that the Bucks wanted to shut him down for the remainder of the season and that he refused . Doc Rivers said last week that Antetokounmpo had not even practiced, though team sources indicate he has been conducting increasingly intense pregame workouts before home games as a show of readiness. The National Basketball Players Association issued a public rebuke of Milwaukee late last month, with a statement implying the franchise is tanking and damaging the league's integrity. The Bucks were eliminated from playoff contention on March 28 , marking their first postseason absence since 2016. Antetokounmpo, 31, has appeared in just 36 games this season, by far the fewest of his career, dealing with two calf strains, an adductor strain and the knee hyperextension. Milwaukee has five regular-season games remaining and currently have the 10th best lottery odds . Antetokounmpo is expected to be traded by the Bucks this offseason if he declines to commit to a five-year, $275 million extension he becomes eligible to sign in October.

title: South Carolina Ends UConn's Perfect Season, Auriemma-Staley Exchange Caps Heated Game
summary: South Carolina exacted revenge on UConn on Friday, stunning the undefeated Huskies 62-48 in the Women's Final Four in Phoenix to advance to its third straight national championship game. The victory was punctuated by a heated postgame confrontation between coaches Geno Auriemma and Dawn Staley, after which Auriemma walked off the court without shaking hands with Staley or any South Carolina player or coach. The Gamecocks had been reminded of last year's national championship loss to UConn every single morning since the offseason began. Sports performance coach Molly Binetti kept that final score displayed on weight room TVs by 6 a.m. daily, instilling the sting of defeat even in newcomers. "We saw that in our faces, so even though I didn't experience that, I had to take on that because I knew that it was just more than just myself," said Ta'Niya Latson, who joined the program this season. "It was for the program, for the team and for the coaches." South Carolina delivered on that motivation by dismantling an offense that had averaged 87 points per game this season. AP Player of the Year Sarah Strong was held to just 11 points. All-American Azzi Fudd managed eight points on 3-of-15 shooting. UConn shot just 31 percent from the field overall, ending its bid for a seventh undefeated championship season and finishing 38-1. The Gamecocks didn't shoot much better at 37 percent, but their defensive physicality created problems throughout. South Carolina trailed by just two at halftime despite making only four shots in the second quarter. The Gamecocks shot 46 percent from the field and converted eight of 10 free throws to build a 10-point lead with three minutes remaining in the third quarter. UConn responded with three consecutive three-pointers, but South Carolina answered with four straight points to take a five-point cushion into the fourth quarter, never looking back. The frustration boiled over during a third-quarter timeout when Auriemma, already agitated, spoke directly to ESPN's Holly Rowe on the broadcast. "There were six fouls called that quarter, all of them against us," Auriemma said. "And they've been beating the s- out of our guys down there the entire game. And I'm not making excuses because we haven't been able to make a shot. But this is ridiculous. Their coach ran some rage on the sideline and called the referees some names you don't want to hear. And now we get 6 to 0, and I got a kid with a ripped jersey. And they go, 'I didn't see it.' C'mon man, this is the national championship." The ripped jersey belonged to Strong, who replaced her torn No. 21 with a blank No. 55 before returning to the floor. She was measured in her postgame explanation. "It was an accident," Strong said. "I missed that shot. Ripped it by accident." South Carolina finished 18-of-22 from the free-throw line. UConn converted just four of six attempts and shot 29 percent from three. As the final seconds expired, Auriemma crossed the sideline toward Staley in what appeared to be a routine postgame exchange. Instead both coaches became visibly heated. Assistant coaches moved between them before Auriemma walked directly to the locker room, bypassing the handshake line entirely and leaving before his own players. Staley told Rowe she was uncertain what prompted the confrontation. "I have no idea," Staley said. "But imma let you know this: I'm of integrity. I'm of integrity. So if I did something wrong to Geno, I had no idea what I did. I guess he thought I didn't shake his hand at the beginning of the game. I didn't know, I went down there pregame, shook everybody on his staff's hand, I don't know what he came with after the game. But hey, sometimes things get heated, we move on." The Gamecocks advance to Sunday's national championship game, extending one of the most dominant runs in women's college basketball history under Staley, who has now guided South Carolina to four title game appearances in five seasons.

title: USC's JuJu Watkins Back On Court At USA Basketball Camp, Expected Ready For Next Season
summary: JuJu Watkins is making steady progress in her return from a torn right ACL, shooting on the practice court at the Phoenix Mercury facility during USA Basketball national team training camp without a knee brace. The Southern California star, who lost her entire 2025-26 season to the injury, expects to be ready for the start of next college season. "Recovery's coming along very well," Watkins said. "Just staying with it, and I'll be back soon. Getting closer to the finish line, so I'm grateful about that." Watkins tore her ACL in the second round of the 2025 NCAA Tournament against Mississippi State on March 24, leaving her more than a year removed from the injury. Her participation at the current Phoenix camp marks an upgrade from December's training camp at Duke, where she watched entirely from the sidelines. "I think this journey has definitely taught me to have a lot of patience," Watkins said. "So many ups and downs within the process, but just staying true to myself and staying true to the process." Team USA managing director Sue Bird offered an encouraging assessment of Watkins' trajectory. "JuJu's in a great spot," Bird said. "Just in talking to her, you can see she's in a really settled place, a really calm place. Ready to get back on the court. As you get closer to the end, it actually gets harder because you know you're ready, but you still have to take your time with it. But she looks great." Watkins earned AP Player of the Year honors in 2024-25 and was a two-time All-American in her first two seasons. The No. 1 recruit in the 2023 class, she watched USC finish 18-14 and fall to South Carolina in the second round of this year's NCAA Tournament without her. "A lot of ways we can improve. A lot of things we do well," Watkins said of observing the Trojans from the sideline. "Just being able to see things from a different perspective has definitely helped and given me some things I can continue to contribute to the team next year." USC enters next season with the nation's top recruiting class, headlined by No. 1 recruit Saniyah Hall. Coach Lindsay Gottlieb has landed the top-ranked player in the country in three of the past four recruiting cycles.

title: UConn's Sarah Strong Named AP Women's Basketball Player Of The Year
summary: Sarah Strong was named the Associated Press women's basketball Player of the Year Thursday, becoming just the fifth sophomore in history to claim the honor after guiding UConn to an undefeated 38-0 record and a Final Four berth. Strong received 25 of 31 votes from the national media panel, with Vanderbilt's Mikayla Blakes earning four votes and UCLA's Lauren Betts collecting the remaining two. Strong joins an elite group of sophomores to win the award since its 1995 inception, including Oklahoma's Courtney Paris (2007), UConn's Maya Moore (2009) and Breanna Stewart (2014), and USC's JuJu Watkins, who claimed the honor last season. It is the 13th time a Huskies player has won, with Paige Bueckers being the last before Strong in 2021. "Anybody that's watched us play would probably tell you that she's the heart and soul of our team," UConn coach Geno Auriemma said. "She elevates the play of everyone on our team." Auriemma, who has coached Breanna Stewart, Sue Bird, Maya Moore and Diana Taurasi, placed Strong firmly in that company. "The great ones, the great ones all had it. I never saw Taurasi nervous," he said. "You know that they came in as freshmen and you can tell by the look in their eyes, 'I can handle this.' She's ready for that one." Auriemma described Strong's defining quality as an uncommon composure. "You don't see that look in her eyes, you know, like, oh, my God, there's always just a calmness," Auriemma said. "That allows her to just be free and fluid and play without worry." Strong is averaging 18.6 points, 7.6 rebounds, 3.4 steals and 1.6 blocks per game in just 27 minutes of action. She is shooting 59.4 percent from the field, 40.4 percent from three and 84.8 percent from the free-throw line. Against top-25 opponents, she elevated further, averaging over 20 points and 10 rebounds per game. Strong reached 1,000 career points in her 59th game, the third-fastest player in program history to hit the mark. She also earned Big East Player of the Year honors and was named Most Outstanding Player of the Fort Worth Regional.

title: Kristaps Porzingis Keeping Options Open In Free Agency
summary: Kristaps Porzingis is keeping his options open this offseason despite finding comfort with the Golden State Warriors, declining to commit to a return when asked directly about his future with the franchise. "It's hard to say," Porzingis told The Athletic after Thursday's 118-111 loss to Cleveland. "Of course, it would be nice for me to go ahead and say, 'Yes, I want to continue here,' and this and that. But the reality is I didn't have a good year at all. I barely showed what I'm capable of. And so I have to see what's out there." The 30-year-old big man, acquired from the Atlanta Hawks before February's trade deadline, is averaging 17.5 points, 4.8 rebounds and 2.4 assists in 12 games with Golden State. He missed two weeks with an illness after debuting February 19, an absence he acknowledged was at least partially connected to his postural orthostatic tachycardia syndrome diagnosis. Since returning March 7, he has remained in the lineup. Porzingis is in the final year of a two-year, $60 million contract extension originally signed with the Boston Celtics. He has earned well over $200 million across his career. One factor that could influence his decision is his relationship with Warriors vice president of player health Rick Celebrini, whom Porzingis has called the best in the business. "I believe I'm in incredible hands here," Porzingis said. "When you have a really strong staff with somebody like me, maybe that's had some injuries throughout their career, to be in the best hands, it makes a difference for me to stay out there healthy." Porzingis also cited his eagerness to finally share the floor with Stephen Curry, who is expected to return as early as Sunday against the Houston Rockets, as a factor worth considering. "Who knows? Maybe we get out there and we can't play together," Porzingis said with a smile. "I don't know. Steph doesn't like to play with me - who knows? So obviously, I have to take that into account. But I think, with the experience that he has and, obviously, myself now at this point in my career, we will click." Gui Santos expressed excitement about what the pairing could produce. "You've got Porzingis, who can do whatever he wants in the paint. You've got Steph, who can do whatever he wants in the whole court," Santos said. "I don't know who can guard that." Despite the uncertainty about his future, Porzingis has embraced his time in the Bay Area. "Honestly, the team is great here," he said. "The team is great, the city is great, the organization is fantastic. The only thing is the time difference with Europe - it's 10 hours, it's too much. But everything else, I enjoy it here."

title: Luka Doncic Out For Regular Season With Grade 2 Hamstring Strain, Playoff Status Uncertain
summary: Los Angeles Lakers star Luka Doncic will miss the remainder of the regular season after an MRI Friday revealed a Grade 2 left hamstring strain. Doncic suffered the injury in the third quarter of Thursday's 139-96 loss to the Oklahoma City Thunder in Dallas. A Grade 2 strain typically requires approximately one month of recovery. The Lakers did not provide a timetable for his return beyond the final five regular-season games, leaving his availability for the playoffs in question. The injury also pushes Doncic below the 65-game threshold required for end-of-season award eligibility. His agent, Bill Duffy of WME Basketball, told ESPN's Shams Charania that he will file an "Extraordinary Circumstances Challenge" on his client's behalf. "This season, Luka Doncic has performed at a historic level, leading the league in scoring, carrying the Lakers to third place in the Western Conference and placing himself in the middle of one of the most tightly contested MVP races in memory," Duffy said in a statement. "To ensure that Luka's incredible accomplishments this season are rightly honored and he can be considered for the league's end-of-season awards, we intend to apply for an 'Extraordinary Circumstances Challenge' to the 65-game rule." Duffy cited Doncic's early return from Slovenia following the December 4 birth of his second child as additional context for the filing. Doncic was back competing for the Lakers on December 6. In 64 appearances this season, Doncic leads the NBA with 33.5 points per game and ranks third in assists (8.3), second among guards in rebounds (7.7) and sixth in steals (1.6). He finished a distant fourth in ESPN's final NBA MVP straw poll of 100 media members , trailing Shai Gilgeous-Alexander, Victor Wembanyama and Nikola Jokic. Los Angeles holds the No. 3 seed in the Western Conference, one game ahead of Denver and 2.5 ahead of Houston, with tiebreakers secured against both clubs.

title: Cameron Boozer Becomes Fifth Freshman To Win AP Player Of The Year
summary: Cameron Boozer has been named the Associated Press men's college basketball Player of the Year , becoming only the fifth freshman to win the award and the second straight Duke player to claim the honor following Cooper Flagg last season. Boozer received 59 of 61 votes from AP Top 25 voters, with BYU's AJ Dybantsa collecting the remaining two. Boozer joins fellow Blue Devils star Cooper Flagg last year, another Duke player in Zion Williamson (2019), Kentucky's Anthony Davis (2012) and Texas' Kevin Durant (2007) as freshmen to win the AP award. Each went No. 1 or No. 2 in the NBA draft that year. "It just goes to show more about what our team has done, just because I think that really helps awards like this, having great team success," Boozer told the AP. "It's really just not me." The 6-foot-9, 250-pound forward averaged 22.5 points and 10.2 rebounds per game this season while shooting 55.6 percent overall and 39.1 percent from three. He finished tied for the national lead with 22 double-doubles and added 4.1 assists per game, helping Duke reach No. 1 in the AP Top 25 and earn the top overall NCAA Tournament seed before falling in the Elite Eight. Boozer joins a distinguished group of freshmen AP honorees that includes Flagg, Zion Williamson, Anthony Davis and Kevin Durant. Each of those players was selected No. 1 or No. 2 in the NBA draft that year. "I'm very grateful just that I'm even in those conversations," Boozer said. "Sometimes you've got to take a step back and just remember that once upon a time, you were a kid dreaming to be here." Duke associate head coach Chris Carrawell placed Boozer in elite company. "We've been fortunate enough the last two years to have two of the best freshmen to ever play in college basketball back to back," Carrawell said. "And Cam is right up there." Michigan coach Dusty May offered perhaps the most precise assessment of Boozer's impact. "His desire to rebound the ball, to set physical screens, to play to his advantages, is as impressive as any freshman that I can recall," May said. Boozer is the ninth different Duke player to win the AP award, giving the Blue Devils more winners than any program in the country. UCLA is the next closest with five.

title: A'ja Wilson Confirms Return To Aces On 600 Percent Raise Supermax
summary: A'ja Wilson confirmed Friday she will return to the Las Vegas Aces for the 2026 WNBA season, ending any speculation about the league's best player testing free agency. Wilson made the announcement at USA Basketball training camp. "I love Vegas, I'm not leaving Vegas," Wilson said. "I'm not looking anywhere, I'm looking to win another one, I'm looking to defend a championship that we have in Las Vegas." Wilson, an unrestricted free agent, is expected to sign a supermax contract worth $1.4 million this season under the new collective bargaining agreement, per the Las Vegas Review-Journal. She earned $200,000 in 2025. The 28-year-old has won three championships, four MVP awards, three Defensive Player of the Year trophies and two WNBA Finals MVP honors across eight seasons with Las Vegas. She led the league in scoring last season at 23.4 points per game and added 10.2 rebounds per game. Wilson's return anchors a roster that will require significant reconstruction. The Aces currently have just one player under contract and must re-sign Chelsea Gray, Jackie Young and other key contributors from their title-winning core. The franchise also faces potential losses in Friday's WNBA expansion draft, where it could forfeit rights to up to two players. Las Vegas retained its full coaching staff and added former Dallas Wings assistant Nola Henry to the bench this offseason. Wilson is also competing with Team USA this week as the national team pursues a fifth consecutive World Cup gold medal. She was part of the last two gold-medal-winning squads, including a standout 2022 run in which she joined the team midway through the tournament after delivering an Aces championship.

title: Jason Kidd Refutes Mark Cuban's Claims On Luka Doncic Trade, Says He Was 'Informed At 11th Hour'
summary: Dallas Mavericks coach Jason Kidd directly disputed minority owner Mark Cuban's assertion that he played a role in the February 2025 trade that sent Luka Doncic to the Los Angeles Lakers, stating Friday he was kept out of the process entirely until moments before the deal became public. "Cuban has mentioned that I knew about the trade," Kidd said. "Unfortunately, as I have said, I was not part of the process. I was informed at the 11th hour. And that's the truth." Kidd provided specific details on the sequence of events. Former general manager Nico Harrison summoned him to a room and told him a trade would go public at 11 p.m. News of the Doncic deal broke at 11:12 p.m. CT on February 1, 2025. Cuban's accusation came earlier this week on the "Intersections" podcast, where the former majority owner said the trade that dismantled a contending roster fell on the coach and front office. "That doesn't justify it for our coach and general manager to stand up and trade our best player," Cuban said. Kidd said he called Cuban immediately after hearing the podcast comments. "I called Mark right away," Kidd said. "We had that conversation. That's not a public conversation. But understanding that Mark, again, has done a lot for the Dallas Mavericks. And for the city. And for myself. This is not about 'He said, she said.' It's about his opinion. And I'm here to tell you the truth." Dallas has struggled significantly since the trade. Harrison was fired in November amid a 3-8 start, and the franchise is now searching for a new head of basketball operations ahead of this year's draft. Doncic, meanwhile, is averaging 33.5 points per game and leads the NBA in scoring for the Lakers, who sit third in the Western Conference. Kidd, entering his sixth season on the Dallas sideline, redirected focus toward rebuilding around rookie forward Cooper Flagg. "We have planted our flag," Kidd said. "That's Cooper Flagg. We have an opportunity to build around him. It's an exciting time." Cuban sold his majority stake to Miriam Adelson and Patrick Dumont in December 2023 for $3.5 billion. Kidd, who won a championship under Cuban as a player in 2011, was careful to separate their shared history from the current dispute. "I played for Mark. Won a championship. He's one of the greatest owners I've played for," Kidd said. "Understanding roles have changed."

title: Nebraska's Fred Hoiberg Named AP Men's Basketball Coach Of The Year
summary: Fred Hoiberg was named the Associated Press men's basketball coach of the year Friday after guiding Nebraska to a 28-win season, the program's first NCAA Tournament victory and a Sweet 16 appearance. Hoiberg received 17 votes from a 61-person media panel, edging Duke's Jon Scheyer (13 votes) to become the Big Ten's first national coach of the year since Michigan's Juwan Howard in 2021. Arizona's Tommy Lloyd received 11 votes. "It took us some time to get here, but it was all about getting the right players in here, especially the ones that the fans could get behind," Hoiberg said. The Cornhuskers set a school record with 15 Big Ten wins and matched the program record of 26 victories before March Madness even began. Nebraska posted its first tournament win in nine all-time tries with a 76-47 victory over Troy before falling to Iowa in the Sweet 16. "When the sting does wear off, which it will at some point, maybe, these guys deserve a lot of credit for what they have done for Nebraska basketball," Hoiberg said. The turnaround carries deep personal significance. Hoiberg was born in Lincoln, his parents are Nebraska graduates, his maternal grandfather Jerry Bush coached the Cornhuskers from 1953 to 1963, and his paternal grandfather Otto Hoiberg taught at the school for 30 years. When Hoiberg took over in 2019, Nebraska had reached the tournament once in 21 seasons. His first two years produced a combined 7-45 record before the program found its footing. Led by Iowa transfer Pryce Sandfort, Dutch big man Rienk Mast and senior point guard Sam Hoiberg, Fred's son, Nebraska won its first 20 games and reached No. 5 in the AP Top 25, the program's highest-ever ranking. Scheyer finished runner-up after Duke claimed the No. 1 ranking for four straight weeks and earned a No. 1 NCAA Tournament seed. He adjusted his approach from last year's Final Four run, building around star freshman Cameron Boozer's interior dominance. Lloyd, the 2022 AP coach of the year in his first Arizona season, guided the Wildcats to nine weeks at No. 1 and their first Final Four since 2001.

title: Tommy Lloyd Agrees To New Five-Year Deal With Arizona
summary: Headline Options: Tommy Lloyd Signs New Five-Year Deal With Arizona, Spurns North Carolina Interest Tommy Lloyd Commits To Arizona With New Deal Worth $7.5M Annually: "I'm Staying" Tommy Lloyd ended days of uncertainty Friday, announcing he has signed a new five-year contract with Arizona and will not pursue the North Carolina head coaching vacancy. Lloyd will average $7.5 million annually over the life of the fully guaranteed deal, starting at nearly $7.2 million in 2026-27, making him one of the five highest-paid coaches in college basketball, per ESPN sources. "I'm staying at Arizona," Lloyd said at a Friday news conference. "Arizona basketball, you guys know what it means to me, and when I say it's a special place, that always comes from the bottom of my heart." Lloyd addressed the persistent speculation about Michael Jordan personally recruiting him to Chapel Hill, calling the rumored phone calls fiction, before delivering a pointed declaration of loyalty. "I made a decision that my Michael Jordan is Steve Kerr, and I'm proud to be an Arizona Wildcat," he said. The agreement includes significant performance bonuses and an expanded staff salary pool. The buyout to leave for another program, which stood at $9 million under his previous contract, is expected to remain substantial in both directions. Lloyd's commitment arrives at the peak of his tenure in Tucson. He is 148-35 in five seasons, the most wins by any head coach in NCAA history through their first five seasons. Arizona swept the Big 12 regular-season and conference tournament titles this year and advanced to the program's first Final Four since 2001, where the Wildcats face Michigan on Saturday. Athletic director Desiree Reed-Francois was direct in her assessment. "Tommy Lloyd is the best coach in college basketball, and we have a strong conviction in the future of Arizona Basketball under his leadership," she said in a statement. Senior guard Jaden Bradley echoed that sentiment from the locker room. "Excited that he signed back with Arizona," Bradley said. "I know the fan base is excited as well." Since arriving from Gonzaga in 2021, where he spent two decades as Mark Few's top assistant, Lloyd has guided Arizona to three conference regular-season titles and four Sweet 16 appearances in five NCAA tournaments.
